event: Cro-Magnon humans inhabit the region and create the cave paintings of Chauvet.
The Lascaux cave paintings are created in the Dordogne region.
Greek colonists from Phocaea found Massalia (Marseille), the oldest city in France.
Julius Caesar begins the Gallic Wars, leading to the Roman conquest of Gaul.
Vercingetorix surrenders to Caesar at Alesia, ending major Gallic resistance.
Christian martyrs are executed at Lyon, an early sign of Christianity's spread in Gaul.
Roman and Visigothic forces defeat Attila the Hun at the Catalaunian Plains.
Clovis I becomes king of the Franks, founding the Merovingian dynasty.
Clovis I converts to Catholic Christianity after his victory at Tolbiac.
Charles Martel halts the Umayyad advance at the Battle of Tours.
Charlemagne is crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III.
The Treaty of Verdun divides the Carolingian Empire, creating West Francia.
Hugh Capet is elected king, founding the long-ruling Capetian dynasty.
Duke William of Normandy conquers England, becoming William the Conqueror.
Pope Urban II launches the First Crusade at the Council of Clermont.
The Albigensian Crusade begins against the Cathars in southern France.
Philip II Augustus wins the Battle of Bouvines, consolidating royal power.
The papacy relocates to Avignon, beginning nearly seventy years of French influence.
The Hundred Years' War with England begins over claims to the French throne.
The Black Death arrives, killing a large share of the French population.
Joan of Arc lifts the Siege of Orleans, turning the tide of the Hundred Years' War.
The Hundred Years' War ends in French victory, expelling the English from most of France.
Francis I wins at Marignano and ushers in the French Renaissance.
The French Wars of Religion erupt between Catholics and Protestant Huguenots.
The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre kills thousands of Huguenots in Paris and beyond.
Henry IV issues the Edict of Nantes, granting religious tolerance to Protestants.
Louis XIV, the Sun King, begins his 72-year reign defining French absolutism.
Louis XIV moves the royal court to the Palace of Versailles.
Louis XIV revokes the Edict of Nantes, prompting mass Huguenot emigration.
The storming of the Bastille ignites the French Revolution.
The First French Republic is proclaimed, abolishing the monarchy.
King Louis XVI is executed by guillotine, followed by the Reign of Terror.
Napoleon Bonaparte seizes power in a coup, becoming First Consul.
Napoleon crowns himself Emperor and promulgates the Napoleonic Code.
Napoleon is defeated at Waterloo, ending his empire and restoring the monarchy.
The July Revolution overthrows Charles X and installs a constitutional monarchy.
The February Revolution establishes the Second Republic with universal male suffrage.
Napoleon III proclaims the Second French Empire.
Defeat in the Franco-Prussian War costs Alsace-Lorraine and founds the Third Republic.
The Eiffel Tower is completed for the Paris World's Fair.
The Dreyfus Affair erupts, exposing deep divisions over antisemitism and justice.
France enshrines the separation of church and state through its laicite law.
France enters World War I, halting the German advance at the First Battle of the Marne.
The Battle of Verdun becomes one of the longest and deadliest battles of World War I.
World War I ends with an armistice, returning Alsace-Lorraine to France.
Nazi Germany invades and occupies France, with the collaborationist Vichy regime in the south.
Allied forces land at Normandy on D-Day and liberate Paris from German occupation.
France becomes a founding UN member with a permanent Security Council seat.
The Fourth Republic is established and French women vote in their first general elections.
France withdraws from Indochina after Dien Bien Phu as the Algerian War begins.
France signs the Treaty of Rome, co-founding the European Economic Community.
Charles de Gaulle founds the Fifth Republic with a strengthened presidency.
France detonates its first atomic bomb, becoming a nuclear power.
Algeria gains independence after a brutal war, ending France's largest colony.
Mass student and worker protests in May nearly topple the government.
Francois Mitterrand becomes the first Socialist president of the Fifth Republic.
France adopts euro banknotes and coins, retiring the franc.
Coordinated terrorist attacks in Paris, including at the Bataclan, kill 130 people.
A major fire severely damages the Notre-Dame de Paris cathedral.
Paris hosts the Summer Olympic Games for the third time.
